topic.title: Core List Types
topic.description: HTML provides ol for sequenced items and ul for unordered groups, both built from li child elements.

content.text: Core List Types: ol, ul, and li

text.value: HTML provides two core list containers. The
(ordered list) element wraps a sequence of items where order conveys meaning: steps, rankings, instructions. The
(unordered list) element wraps a set of items where order is incidental: features, ingredients, navigation links. Both containers require
is the only valid direct child of
. Lists can be nested to any depth by placing a new
. Both list types map to the
. Screen readers announce the list type (ordered or unordered), the item count, and the current position within the list. Reference:

rows: Every item in ol or ul
Countdown lists, top-N rankings in reverse
Resuming a list split across content

content.title: list-style: none Removes Semantics in Safari VoiceOver
content.text: Applying list-style: none to a ul element causes Safari with VoiceOver to no longer announce it as a list. Apple made this intentional change to prevent decorative lists from being announced. If the list is semantically a list (navigation, steps, items), add role='list' explicitly after removing the default bullet style. This is the single most common accessibility regression in CSS resets and navigation components.

examples.title: Ordered and Unordered Lists with All Attributes
examples.description: This example shows an ol step-by-step tutorial with the type, start, and reversed attributes, a ul feature list with nested sub-lists, the accessible navigation pattern using ul with role='list' after list-style: none, and a comparison of correct versus incorrect list markup. All examples include inline comments explaining every attribute decision.
examples.code_snippet:
Core List Types

1. Ordered list: installation steps

  1. Install Node.js from nodejs.org
  2. Open a terminal and run npm install -g @anthropic-ai/cli
  3. Create a project directory and navigate into it
    1. Run mkdir my-project
    2. Run cd my-project
  4. Create a .env file with your API key
  5. Run npm start to launch the application

2. Styled step list (CSS counter-reset)

  1. Clone the repository

    Run git clone https://github.com/devdocs/html-course in your terminal.

  2. Install dependencies

    Run npm install inside the project folder.

  3. Start the dev server

    Run npm run dev and open http://localhost:3000.

3. ol attributes: type, start, reversed

Outline with Roman numerals:

  1. Introduction to HTML
  2. Document structure
  3. Semantic elements

Continuing from step 4 (start="4"):

  1. Configure environment variables
  2. Run database migrations
  3. Deploy to production

Top 3 countdown (reversed):

  1. JavaScript Frameworks
  2. CSS Grid and Flexbox
  3. HTML Accessibility Fundamentals

4. Unordered list: feature list

  • Semantic HTML structure
  • Keyboard accessible components
  • Responsive layouts
    • Mobile-first breakpoints
    • CSS Grid and Flexbox
  • WCAG 2.1 AA compliance

5. Styled feature list (list-style: none + role)

  • Instant feedback on every exercise
  • Certificate of completion
  • Downloadable code examples
  • Lifetime access to course updates

6. Navigation list (safe list-style: none pattern)

  • HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript
  • Accessibility
examples.output: Page renders six list sections. The default ordered list shows numbered steps with a nested lettered sub-list for sub-steps. The styled step list shows blue numbered circle markers built with CSS counters. The attributes section shows three separate ol elements: Roman numerals, a list starting at 4, and a reversed countdown. The feature list shows a default bulleted ul with a nested sub-list. The styled feature list shows green checkmark bullets. The navigation list shows a horizontal flex nav using ul with role='list'.

examples.title: Nested Lists, Document Outlines, and List Accessibility
examples.description: This example builds a deeply nested table of contents with ol elements, a definition-style feature comparison using ul, and a full audit of the ARIA announcements produced by each list pattern. It also shows the common mistake of placing content outside li elements and how browsers recover from it.
examples.code_snippet:
Nested Lists and List Accessibility

1. Nested ol: table of contents

Contents

  1. Introduction
    1. What is HTML
    2. Brief history
  2. Document Structure
    1. DOCTYPE declaration
    2. The head element
    3. The body element
  3. Semantic Elements
    1. Landmark elements
    2. Sectioning content
  4. Forms
  5. Accessibility

2. ol vs ul: side-by-side decision guide

Use ol when order matters

  • Step-by-step instructions
  • Ranked results (1st, 2nd, 3rd)
  • Legal numbered clauses
  • Table of contents
  • Recipe method steps

Use ul when order is incidental

  • Navigation links
  • Product features
  • Ingredient lists
  • Tag clouds
  • Related articles

3. What screen readers announce for each list

Default ul (3 items):
“List, 3 items — Apples — Oranges — Bananas — End of list”

ul with list-style:none but no role="list" (Safari):
“Apples — Oranges — Bananas”
No list announcement, no item count.

ul with list-style:none and role="list" (Safari restored):
“List, 3 items — Apples — Oranges — Bananas — End of list”

Default ol (3 items):
“List, 3 items — 1, Apples — 2, Oranges — 3, Bananas — End of list”

Nested ul inside ul:
“List, 2 items — Fruits — List, 2 items — Apples — Oranges — End of list — Vegetables — End of list”

4. Invalid list markup: wrong vs right

Wrong: text directly in ol/ul, not in li

<ul>
  Free text here  <!-- invalid: text not in li -->
  <li>Item one</li>
  <div>Wrapper</div>  <!-- invalid: div not allowed here -->
  <li>Item two</li>
</ul>

Right: all content inside li elements

<ul>
  <li>Item one</li>
  <li>
    Item two with a wrapper
    <div>Any block content goes inside li</div>
  </li>
</ul>

Wrong: nested list outside li (sibling of li)

<ul>
  <li>Parent item</li>
  <ul>  <!-- invalid: ul is sibling of li, not child -->
    <li>Child item</li>
  </ul>
</ul>

Right: nested list inside li

<ul>
  <li>
    Parent item
    <ul>  <!-- valid: ul is child of li -->
      <li>Child item</li>
    </ul>
  </li>
</ul>
examples.output: Page renders four sections. The table of contents shows a two-level nested ol with decimal outer numbers and lowercase-alpha inner markers, all as anchor links. The decision guide shows a two-column grid comparing ol and ul use cases. The ARIA audit shows five screen reader announcement examples in purple italic text, including the Safari VoiceOver regression. The invalid markup section shows four code blocks alternating red and green for wrong and right nesting and content placement patterns.

when items belong to the same group but order is incidental: navigation links, product features, ingredients, and tag lists can be reshuffled without losing meaning.

List elements provide a semantic grouping that CSS can target precisely with
selectors, enabling dividers, spacing, and marker customisation without wrapper elements.
The fundamental question when choosing between

: if a developer removes the visual bullet markers, the list was probably decorative and should not be announced as a list. This is not an error but a deliberate heuristic. Developers who remove bullet styles for aesthetic reasons on semantic lists must explicitly signal their intent by adding
. The combination of styled appearance and explicit role covers both sighted and non-sighted users correctly.
HTML list elements form a strict parent-child structure.

first. Browsers attempt error recovery for invalid markup, but the recovered DOM may not match the intended structure, producing unexpected visual and accessibility results.

structure. This separates visual numbering from HTML semantics, letting assistive tools still read the correct ordered sequence.

examples.title: ol Attributes: type, start, reversed, and li value
examples.description: This example shows all ol attributes in practical contexts: a numbered tutorial with nested alpha sub-steps, a legal document outline with Roman numerals, a split tutorial that resumes numbering with start, a top-3 countdown with reversed, and the li value attribute for skipping numbers in a list.
examples.code_snippet:
Ordered List: All Attributes

1. Default ol (type="1", decimal)

  1. Open the terminal application
  2. Navigate to your project directory
  3. Run the install command
  4. Start the development server

2. Nested ol with type="a" sub-steps

  1. Set up the environment
    1. Install Node.js from nodejs.org
    2. Verify with node --version
    3. Install npm dependencies with npm install
  2. Configure the application
    1. Copy .env.example to .env
    2. Fill in the API key and database credentials
  3. Run the tests with npm test

3. Roman numerals (type="i") for legal outline

  1. Definitions and Interpretation

4. start attribute: resuming a split list

Steps 1 to 3 are described above. Continuing from step 4:

  2. Run the smoke test suite

5. reversed: countdown list

examples.output: Page renders six ordered list examples. The default decimal list shows four numbered setup steps. The nested list shows two outer steps, each with inner alpha sub-steps (a. b. c.), with the outer numbering continuing after the nested list. The Roman numeral list shows five legal clause headings. The start='4' list continues from step 4 inside a light-grey card. The reversed list shows three courses counted 3. 2. 1. The li value list shows items 1, 2, 5, 6 — skipping 3 and 4 — with a note on the skipped step.

text.value: with the reader: the numbering is meaningful information, not decoration. When a user hears 'step 3 of 6' from a screen reader, they know they are two-thirds through a process. When a developer uses
for a list that is actually unordered (such as a feature list), they break this contract, suggesting a sequence that does not exist.
initialises the counter before any items.
attribute controls how the counter value is rendered (decimal, alpha, Roman) without changing the underlying integer value, which is what the
attribute for controlling marker style in modern projects. CSS
supports all the same values plus
attribute is presentational and overridden by CSS, so CSS is the single source of truth for visual style.

subtopics.title: Unordered List Elements
subtopics.description: The ul element groups unordered items and needs role='list' when list-style: none is applied.

text.value: where order carries no inherent meaning. The default visual marker is a filled disc bullet, controllable via CSS
. Common use cases include navigation menus, feature lists, ingredient lists, and tag groups. When
is applied, Safari with VoiceOver stops announcing the element as a list. Adding
ARIA role. This requirement applies to

content.title: Navigation Is Always a ul
content.text: Navigation links are almost universally implemented as a ul inside a nav element. The ul provides the list semantics (item count, current position) and nav provides the navigation landmark. Styling the list with list-style: none and flexbox or grid for layout is correct and expected — just remember to add role='list' to the ul to preserve VoiceOver semantics.

examples.title: Unordered Lists: Navigation, Features, and Nested Groups
examples.description: This example shows the ul element for a navigation menu with list-style: none and role='list', a feature checklist with custom CSS markers, a nested ul for a site menu with sub-menus, and a tag group. Each example includes the role='list' pattern and inline comments explaining the Safari VoiceOver requirement.

examples.output: Page renders five unordered list patterns. The horizontal navigation shows a dark navbar with four white-on-dark links. The vertical sidebar nav shows a bordered list with five module links. The feature list shows five items with green checkmarks instead of bullets. The nested menu shows three levels of indentation with disc, circle, and square bullet markers. The tag group shows six blue pill badges in a flex-wrapping horizontal row.

seo_metadata.meta_title: HTML Core List Types | ol, ul, li Elements Complete Guide
seo_metadata.meta_description: Master HTML list elements: ordered list ol with type, start, reversed attributes, unordered list ul with role='list' for accessibility, and nested list patterns.
